topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the flat, unfeeling finality of a closing door. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party for Sarah has been cancelled." I reread the sentence three times, the words blurring slightly. My hand hovered over the keyboard, the cursor blinking mockingly on the blank document. I stood by the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, each drop mirroring a slow, heavy ache in my chest. The planned evening, the carefully constructed anticipation—it all dissolved into a damp, grey mess. I felt a peculiar inertia take hold; my feet seemed weighted, unwilling to move toward the next necessary step. I just stared at the streetlights, the silence of my apartment amplifying the hollow echo where excitement used to reside.

[story 2]

"No, this can't be right." I whispered the words into the empty room, my voice raspy. I scrolled through the thread one last time, searching for a typo, a misplaced message, anything to suggest this was a mistake. The reality settled over me like a thick blanket, suffocating. I walked to the kitchen, gripping the edge of the counter until my knuckles turned white. The silence of the house was oppressive; every shadow seemed to lean in, expectant. I began pacing, a restless energy churning beneath my skin, but it wasn't energy for action—it was just frantic, undirected movement. I stopped abruptly, leaning my forehead against the cool granite, feeling utterly immobilized, a statue carved from sudden disappointment.

[story 3]

The phone call had ended minutes ago, leaving a ringing silence in its wake. I sat on the floor, surrounded by discarded wrapping paper from decorations I had already started assembling. My fingers traced the patterns on the glossy paper, the texture offering no comfort. A strange numbness spread through my limbs, making simple tasks—like picking up a dropped napkin—feel monumental. I looked around the room, at the half-inflated balloons mocking the void where laughter should have been. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but the sound caught in my throat, trapped behind a sudden, heavy pressure. I just lay there, perfectly still, waiting for the world to somehow reorient itself around this sudden, inexplicable lack of momentum.
